items.question: What is a vector space, and why is it useful in machine learning?
items.answer: A vector space is a set of vectors equipped with addition and scalar multiplication that satisfies the standard vector space axioms. It is useful in machine learning because many objects of interest (feature vectors, embeddings, activations) live in vector spaces, so we can use linear algebra tools such as distances, projections, subspaces and basis changes to analyze models and data.

items.question: How do you test whether a subset U of a vector space V is a subspace of V?
items.answer: To show that U is a subspace of V, you must check three properties: the zero vector of V is in U, U is closed under addition (if u1 and u2 are in U then u1 + u2 is in U), and U is closed under scalar multiplication (if u is in U and c is a scalar then c*u is in U). If any of these conditions fails, U is not a subspace.

items.question: Explain what it means for vectors v1, ..., vk to be linearly independent.
items.answer: Vectors v1, ..., vk are linearly independent if the only way to form the zero vector as a linear combination c1*v1 + ... + ck*vk is to take all coefficients c1, ..., ck equal to 0. If there exists a non-zero choice of coefficients that gives the zero vector, the vectors are linearly dependent.

items.question: What does it mean for a set of vectors to form a basis of R^n?
items.answer: A set of vectors forms a basis of R^n if the vectors are linearly independent and their span is all of R^n. Equivalently, every vector in R^n can be written in exactly one way as a linear combination of the basis vectors.

items.question: Why is understanding subspaces important when working with techniques like PCA?
items.answer: PCA selects a low-dimensional subspace of the original feature space that captures most of the variance in the data. Understanding subspaces clarifies that PCA is choosing a new basis for a smaller subspace and that projections, reconstruction error and explained variance are all statements about how data relates to that subspace.
